title: The effect of yoga on sleep quality and insomnia in women with sleep problems: a systematic review and meta-analysis
headline: The effect of yoga on sleep quality and insomnia in women with sleep problems: a systematic review and meta-an
summary: A meta-analysis / systematic review of yoga examined sleep. Yoga was not associated with any serious adverse events. The moderator analyses suggest that participants in the non-breast cancer subgroup and participants in the non-peri/postmenopausal subgroup were associated with greater benefits, with a direct correlation of total class time with quality of sleep among other related benefits.
keyFindings: Four RCTs revealed no evidence for the effects of yoga compared with the control group in improving the sleep quality for peri/postmenopausal women using PSQI (SMD = - 0.31; 95% CI = - 0.95 to 0.33; P = 0.34).
Yoga was not associated with any serious adverse events.

The moderator analyses suggest that participants in the non-breast cancer subgroup and participants in the non-peri/postmenopausal subgroup were associated with greater benefits, with a direct correlation of total class time with quality of sleep among other related benefits.
limitations: Two reviewers independently evaluated risk of bias by using the risk of bias tool suggested by the Cochrane Collaboration for programming and conducting systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
Pooled estimates combine heterogeneous studies; effect sizes vary by practice, population, and control condition.
The practice studied (yoga) is not the Miracle of Mind practice; findings do not transfer directly to MoM.

Yoga was not associated with any serious adverse events. The moderator analyses suggest that participants in the non-breast cancer subgroup and participants in the non-peri/postmenopausal subgroup were associated with greater benefits, with a direct correlation of total class time with quality of sleep among other related benefits.
